title: Chapter 30: The Road Downtown
content: We left before dawn, three SUVs crawling through the grey pre-light like beetles through a graveyard.

I rode in the lead vehicle with Sera and Mia. Luna stayed behind—her hybrid biology made her too recognizable for a reconnaissance mission through territory we didn't control. She'd argued, of course. Luna always argued when I left her behind, her amber eyes narrowing in that way that said *I could crush a car door and you're telling me to stay?*
But she'd stayed. Because she trusted me. Because someone had to protect the Compound while the expedition was gone.

Sera sat in the passenger seat, her silver-white hair pulled back in a tight braid, her pale grey eyes scanning the road ahead with the predatory stillness that still unsettled me after three weeks. She'd appeared at the Compound on Day Twenty-Seven—a shadow at the perimeter wall, thin and feral and radiating wrongness the way Luna had when I'd first found her in the hallway.

Unlike Luna, Sera hadn't been a zombie. She'd been something worse: a survivor of Meridian's Phase Two trials, injected with a stabilized LV-7 derivative that had rewritten her cellular architecture without killing her. She was faster than any human had a right to be, strong enough to dent steel, and she moved through combat like water through a crack—finding every weakness, exploiting every gap, leaving nothing but ruin behind.
She was also, inconveniently, fond of terrible puns.
"Road trip," she said, not looking away from the window. "Get it?"
"I got it the first four times you said it."
"Maybe the fifth time it'll be funny."

Mia, driving with one hand on the wheel and her Remington propped against the center console, snorted. "You've been out of isolation for three weeks and you've already corrupted him. That has to be a record."

"I have that effect on people." Sera's smile was sharp, too many teeth, not enough warmth. It wasn't menace—it was habit. Three months alone in a Meridian holding cell while your cells rewrite themselves leaves marks that aren't all physical.

Behind us, the second SUV carried Rick and Jessica. The third, Colt's people—two of his scouts who'd insisted on joining when they heard we were heading downtown. The alliance was still new enough that every joint operation was a test, and Colt didn't trust anything he couldn't observe directly.

The plan was simple: head south through the suburbs, assess the downtown corridor, and determine whether the Roosevelt Building was viable as a forward operating base for Colt's expanding operation. Round trip in twelve hours. In and out. Minimal contact with hostiles.
The apocalypse had a way of making liars out of plans.
The suburbs started three miles south of the Compound.

I'd seen the devastation from the roof—blackened rooflines, overgrown lawns, the occasional shambling figure navigating streets that had once carried school buses and minivans. Seeing it up close was different. The scale of it pressed against your chest like a physical weight.

House after house, block after block, the evidence of collapse accumulated in grim tableaux. A minivan with the driver's door still open, keys in the ignition, a child's car seat in the back coated in dried blood that had faded to rust. A mailbox overflowing with mail that would never be read, the flags still raised in perpetuity. A garden gnome frozen mid-wave on a lawn that had grown knee-high, dandelions pushing through the cracked driveway, nature already beginning the long process of reclamation.
"Stop," Sera said.
Mia braked without question. When Sera said stop, you stopped. It wasn't a request—it was a survival reflex honed in a laboratory where hesitation meant a needle in your arm.

Sera's head tilted, bird-quick, her grey eyes tracking something I couldn't see. Then she was out of the SUV before I could open my mouth, moving with that eerie fluidity that was faster than walking but somehow didn't look like running—a glide across the asphalt that covered thirty feet in the time it took me to unbuckle my seatbelt.
I grabbed the .380 and followed. Mia had the Remington up and her eye to the scope before I'd cleared the door.
Sera was crouched behind an overturned delivery truck, one hand flat on the pavement, her head still tilted. Listening.
"Three," she murmured. "No—four. One's wounded, trailing behind. Northwest, moving parallel to Elm Street. Armed. Not military—they move different. Raiders."

Crane's people. We'd heard the name on Colt's intercepts—Marcus Crane, a warlord who'd consolidated the downtown raider packs into something approaching an army. Extortion, slavery, resource control. The kind of man the apocalypse had been custom-built to produce.
"How far?" I asked.
"Hundred meters and closing. They haven't seen us yet." Her fingers drummed the asphalt—once, twice, three times. A countdown. "I can take them before they radio."

The words were delivered with the same inflection as *road trip*—casual, almost cheerful. But her grey eyes had gone flat, the predatory stillness crystallizing into something harder, colder. She wasn't asking permission. She was informing me of her intention.
"Sera—"

"Three alive means three radio calls means Crane knows we're in his territory within the hour. Three dead means silence." She looked at me, and for a moment I saw the thing Meridian had made—not a person, not a zombie, something in between that had been forged in pain and emerged with killing as its first language. "I'm not asking to kill them, Alex. I'm telling you I'm going to. The only question is whether you want to watch or wait here."

I hated this. I hated the calculus of survival that turned every encounter into a body count. I hated that she was right—raiders who spotted us would report us, and Crane's response time was better than ours.
But mostly I hated that I nodded.
Sera moved.

She was a blur of silver-white and shadow, crossing the distance to the intersection faster than the .380 could track. The first raider didn't have time to scream—she took him from behind, one hand over his mouth, the other twisting his head at an angle necks weren't designed for. A soft crack. He dropped.

The second was turning, rifle coming up, when she was already inside his guard. Her elbow caught him in the throat, collapsing his airway, and as he staggered she relieved him of his sidearm and put two rounds into the third raider before he'd finished reaching for his radio.
Three bodies. Eight seconds.
The fourth—the wounded one trailing behind—staggered around the corner of a laundromat just in time to see Sera standing over his squad. His eyes went wide. His mouth opened.
Sera's thrown knife took him in the throat before the first syllable formed.
Four bodies. Eleven seconds.
She retrieved her knife, wiped it on the last raider's jacket, and walked back to the SUV with the unhurried calm of someone returning from a coffee run.
"Clear," she said. "No radio traffic."
Mia lowered the Remington. Her expression was unreadable, but her grip on the rifle had whitened her knuckles.
We found the manifest in the second raider's pack.

It was a water-stained folder, clipped shut with a rusted binder clip, the pages inside covered in handwritten columns that tracked supplies, personnel movements, and—most critically—intelligence. Crane kept meticulous records. Whether that was military discipline or pathological control, I didn't know and didn't care. What mattered was what was written on the third page.

**NEW EDEN — CONFIRMED LOCATION**
**SOURCE: Interrogated survivor (F), picked up north of I-90 interchange**
**SETTLEMENT SIZE: 40-60 personnel (est.)**
**DEFENSES: Reinforced structure, possible underground access**
**NOTABLE ASSETS: Medical facility (functioning), agriculture (hydroponic), armed perimeter**
**HIGH-VALUE TARGET: Chen, Alex — System Host (Romance variant) — CONFIRMED ACTIVE**
**PRIORITY: Asset acquisition or elimination before Alliance consolidation**
**TIMELINE: Recon-in-force authorized within 14 days**
My blood went cold.
New Eden. That was the Compound's designation in Crane's network—the name his scouts had given our settlement. He knew where we were. He knew *who* I was. He knew about the system.
And he was planning to hit us within two weeks.
"Alex." Mia's voice was tight. She was reading over my shoulder, her tactical mind already spinning through implications. "If he knows about the system—"

"Then he knows what I can do. And what I can't." I stared at the words *asset acquisition or elimination*. Asset first. They wanted me alive, probably for the same reason Meridian had wanted Sera—for what the system could give them. But elimination was the fallback. Clean, efficient, final.

Sera took the manifest from my hands, her grey eyes scanning the contents with the detached precision of someone who'd read her own file in a Meridian lab and learned to process terrible information without flinching.
"Fourteen days," she said. "That's generous. He's confident."
"Or cautious. You don't raid a fortified position without reconnaissance, and our perimeter's been tight."
"Was tight." She tapped the page. "He interrogated someone. Whoever she was, she talked."
Someone from the Compound. Or someone who'd passed close enough to observe our operations. Either way, the leak was a problem I'd have to solve when we got back—assuming we got back.
I looked at the manifest one more time, at the cold bureaucracy of our destruction laid out in pen on stained paper, and felt the watch pulse against my wrist.

**SYSTEM ALERT: THREAT INTELLIGENCE ACQUIRED**
**SOURCE: Crane Raider Manifest**
**THREAT LEVEL: HIGH — Organized hostile force with accurate positional data**
**RECOMMENDATION: Accelerate defensive preparations. Alliance with Command System host now strategically critical.**
**TIMELINE: ~14 days before hostile reconnaissance-in-force**
Fourteen days. The clock was running, and we were still miles from home, driving deeper into territory controlled by a man who wanted to put me in a cage or a grave.
"We keep moving," I said, folding the manifest into my jacket. "Downtown first. Intelligence first. Then we run like hell."
Sera's sharp smile flashed in the grey morning light. "My favorite kind of mission."

She wasn't wrong. But as the SUVs rolled south through the corpse of the suburbs, past burned homes and shattered lives and the slow green creep of a world learning to grow without us, I couldn't shake the feeling that we were already out of time.
The road downtown was paved with the evidence of everything we'd lost.
And somewhere ahead, Marcus Crane was preparing to take what was left.
